data.title: CagriSema Incident: The Surprise Winner And What It Means For The Novo-Lilly Rivalry
data.description:
Intro

When Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) CEO Maziar "Mike" Doustdar and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) CEO David Ricks stood shoulder to shoulder in the White House Oval Office during their drug pricing deal‑announcement ceremony with the Trump administration back in November, they came across almost like close allies fighting for the very same goal.

On that day, the two men helped cement the future landscape for the pricing and accessibility of weight‑loss medications. Moreover, the event likely laid the groundwork for better health outcomes for Americans by supporting healthier weights and lifestyles. Business leaders around the world spend a great deal of effort crafting inspiring mission statements. Yet the kind of foundation that Novo and Lilly laid with that White House event is so powerful that it’s hard to imagine anything better to serve as a great mission.

While companies may come up with big and inspiring mission statements, everyday business reality is tough — any mistake a competitor makes is used right away to gain ground on each other. In sports, the winner is usually determined in a simple and obvious way — someone runs faster, or one team scores more points than the other. In business, however, identifying the winner can be far more complicated. Leadership can shift within months or even weeks, and in many cases, it’s not clear at all who is actually racing ahead.

Great business leaders rely on dozens of essential skills to succeed, and adaptability is certainly one of the most important. One must be able to stand shoulder‑to‑shoulder with their biggest rival in a friendly ceremony one moment, and the next moment fight a tough, unforgiving battle for market leadership with the very same person whose hand they just shook and with whom they exchanged friendly, pleasant words.

If business leadership can be compared to any sport, in my view, the closest match could be chess. Every move can change the entire board, opening countless new possibilities, and the process never stops — a constant, relentless search for the right moves in an ever‑shifting situation.

I see the great duel between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly — and their CEOs — through a sports lens. It’s a sport where the prizes are among the sweetest in the world: one of the largest and fastest‑growing segments in the entire pharmaceutical industry. And the competitors are two of the most outstanding and charismatic CEOs in the sector, leading teams of nearly 70,000 people at Novo Nordisk and around 50,000 at Eli Lilly.

As widely discussed, Lilly is now seen as the clear leader in a two-horse race as the weight-loss market functions in duopoly mode currently — its growth has been much stronger thanks to the incredible success of its hit drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound. That said, Novo has had a new CEO at the helm since last August and is making a serious effort to catch up.

Let’s look at the latest dynamics and how this catch‑up story is progressing, starting with the most recent and impactful event: the announcement of yet another trial on February 23, which sent Novo’s stock down around 15% while Lilly’s rose about 5% on the same day.

The CagriSema Incident

I call it an incident intentionally — because while in principle it was just another trial readout, in reality it turned out to be much more than that. To put it into context, it’s worth recalling the official effectiveness numbers of today’s leading weight-loss drugs. These figures come from the pivotal trials that formed the basis for FDA approval. For injectable Wegovy, the number is 14.9%, for Zepbound it's 20.9%. These figures are not adjusted for the placebo. So, the effectiveness gap favoring Lilly's drug is significant at 6%.

CagriSema is Novo Nordisk’s new drug candidate, intended to play a central role in closing the effectiveness gap known in the market today and helping the company catch up with Lilly. The comparison in this latest "incident" trial was made between overweight patients without diabetes, which means we should look at the potential improvement of Wegovy versus Zepbound, 6% effectiveness gap.

In addition, Lilly has also conducted an additional real‑world study. Published in May last year, it made things look even worse for Novo. In that study, tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Zepbound, showed 20.2% headline effectiveness, while semaglutide 2.4 mg, or Wegovy, fell short of its previously reported figure at just 13.7%, widening the gap to 6.5%.

Lilly has made extensive use of this latest real‑world result to its advantage, promoting it widely whenever possible. The study was also published in the prestigious New England Journal of Medicine. As a result, I believe it has influenced many decisions made by both patients and physicians in real‑world clinical practice. And as we can see, Lilly’s efforts have been highly successful: it has gained substantial market share and reported impressive financial results, while Novo has lost share and growth has slowed sharply.

As I understand it, with the CagriSema REDEFINE 4 trial — which included 809 randomized participants with obesity — Novo aimed to replicate Lilly’s earlier strategic move. Just as the real‑world study that widened the effectiveness gap in Lilly’s favor had been funded and initiated by Lilly, Novo initiated a head‑to‑head CagriSema versus tirzepatide trial. On paper, it was a great idea. The previously conducted CagriSema trial, submitted to the FDA in December, showed an official effectiveness of 22.7%. Tirzepatide, or Zepbound, had delivered weaker results: 20.9% in the FDA‑approval study and 20.2% in Lilly’s own real‑world study.

Given this, it would have been logical to expect CagriSema to deliver a much‑needed win—a clear superiority result that Novo could build on to challenge Lilly’s strong hold on the injectable obesity drug market following potential CagriSema approval later in the year. Instead, although CagriSema performed even slightly better than in the trial supporting the FDA submission, reaching 23%, it still trailed Lilly’s result of 25.5% by two and a half percentage points.

From what I gathered listening to the post‑trial conference call, the outcome came as a genuine shock to Novo’s management. What was expected to be a victory — a strong platform from which to attack Lilly’s powerful leadership position — turned into a disappointment. How this happened remained unclear to management. The only plausible explanation they offered was the open‑label nature of the study, meaning both patients and physicians knew which drug was being administered. This could result in tirzepatide doses being increased more aggressively than CagriSema doses, with many CagriSema patients never even reaching the maximum dose. This might be because doctors, having worked with Lilly’s drug before and being familiar with its side effects, have tended to titrate patients more quickly.

To sum up, this incident in my view could be seen as a major early test for the newly appointed CEO, Mike Doustdar. Although he wasn’t at the helm when the failed trial was designed, he now has to navigate its consequences — and the task ahead of him has just become considerably more challenging.

What's Next

As Novo explained on the post‑trial conference call, the company is not giving up. As expected, they plan to initiate new head‑to‑head trials with CagriSema vs. tirzepatide that could lead to a potentially more favorable outcome for Novo. It seems they have learned the lesson: the next study will be designed as a blinded trial, as it minimizes the risk that different titration schemes could again lead to an unfavorable result.

Another dynamic between the two rivals has been their completely different earnings reports. I have written my analysis of Novo’s results, and Seeking Alpha has covered Lilly’s impressive numbers in detail.

Novo does, however, have some positive developments as well — the uptake of oral Wegovy has been very strong, and this will likely help them regain some ground at a time when the semaglutide–tirzepatide effectiveness gap is clearly working against them. That said, Lilly’s pill - orforglipron is just around the corner, with FDA approval expected on April 10. We will soon see how much of a lead Novo has built in the oral segment and how the oral race accelerates once Lilly enters.

As for the broader competitive landscape, I don’t believe it has become significantly tougher yet. Pfizer (PFE) has reported progress in its GLP-1 programs, and Roche (RHHBY) has highlighted its own achievements. But despite the positive messaging, both companies still have a long road ahead — and no new competition is expected to reach the market before 2028. Even then, those programs would need to advance exceptionally well to pose a real challenge for established leaders.

In the meantime, while it takes years for competitors to reach this highly attractive market, Novo and Lilly have the opportunity to further cement their moats. As discussed earlier in this article, CagriSema could potentially gain market access by the end of the year. And one of the major highlights in the weight‑loss sector this year will be Lilly’s announcement of the final results for retatrutide, which will form the basis for FDA submission. In the best‑case scenario, we could see retatrutide delivering close to 30% weight loss and a potential submission even this year. If that happens, then by the time new entrants attempt to claim their share of the market, Lilly could already have a drug that is likely unchallenged for years to come, as the expected, nearly 30% weight loss sets the bar extraordinarily high.

Valuation

I have previously rated Lilly as Hold and Novo as Buy, and I am maintaining these ratings. While Lilly’s reported earnings were truly impressive — and the outlook equally strong — I believe the current valuation already reflects this strength. I will revisit my stance once we see the upcoming retatrutide readout and the early uptake of orforglipron, as these could provide the conviction needed to consider upgrading Lilly to a Buy.

In Novo’s case, the so‑called CagriSema incident was undeniably negative. Instead of filling the headline effectiveness gap with Lilly, the unfortunate trial outcome has made the situation complicated. That said, the strong uptake of oral Wegovy, along with continued progress in both Zenagamtide and new tri‑agonist programs, combined with attractive fundamentals, gives me enough reason to maintain my Buy rating.

To gain a better perspective, I reviewed peer‑to‑peer comparisons of Novo, Lilly, and other major pharmaceutical companies - Pfizer (PFE), AbbVie (ABBV), Bristol Myers (BMY). The picture is consistent: Lilly trades at significantly higher multiples than the rest of the sector, while Novo’s valuation is roughly in line with peers — slightly better in some multiples, and weaker in others. Novo is clearly trailing only AbbVie, which holds the second‑highest multiples in the group after Lilly.

Risks

I think the main risks for both companies lie in potential failures of their upcoming programs. Eli Lilly’s outlook depends heavily on the forthcoming retatrutide results, and while CagriSema could serve as a bridge for Novo to regain ground against Lilly’s injectables, the next wave of programs will be equally critical for them.

Takeaway

As dynamic as the rivalry between these two giants has become, every week can bring new headlines that shift the picture. As things stand now, Lilly has strong momentum and appears almost unstoppable, while Novo’s new CEO is doing everything he can to close the gap. The unfortunate CagriSema outcome certainly doesn’t make his job any easier.

To me, it seems the market is pricing both companies pretty fairly at the moment. Lilly’s valuation of about $880 billion is more than six times higher than Novo’s $170 billion. The gap is large, but as we know, the market tends to richly reward strong growth and penalize non-growers, so this difference is something we simply have to accept now.

That said, a lot has gone wrong for Novo in recent months, and hopefully, they will find firmer footing again. The strong start of the oral race already gives some reason for optimism.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cintas Just Won't Let UniFirst Go... And That Creates A Catalyst For You
data.description:
March 5th was really interesting day for shareholders of both UniFirst Corporation (UNF) and Cintas Corporation (CTAS). Shares of the former closed up 13.6% after news broke that Cintas is nearing a purchase of the company. If this is giving you a sense of déjà vu, that is understandable. After all, this is not the first time that rumors have circulated about a potential transaction. As recently as December of last year, Cintas was attempting to purchase UniFirst. In fact, I have covered this topic ad nauseam over the last couple of years now. Unfortunately, any price that has been offered up by Cintas has been soundly rejected by UniFirst. But at some point in time, I suspect that a transaction will eventually be agreed upon.

In my last article covering this topic, published in late December of last year, I said that while a transaction under the terms proposed then was unlikely, investors should not give up on UniFirst as an investment prospect. In addition to the potential catalyst of a buyout, which could be done at a rather substantial premium to where the company's share price has traded at, the fact of the matter is that while Cintas looks overpriced to the point that I have called it a Sell candidate in the recent past, UniFirst looks deeply undervalued. And honestly, even though I am not sure about any potential transaction occurring here, I do believe that the picture is strong enough from a fundamental standpoint to justify a Buy rating for UniFirst.

Cintas is serious

On March 5th, rumors began circulating that Cintas is getting close to a deal to acquire UniFirst. According to those rumors, a transaction could be announced as early as next week. And it would be in excess of $275 per share. To understand where that puts us, however, we should dig a bit deeper into the past regarding these two companies. From what I can tell, the first time that Cintas became serious about acquiring UniFirst was back in 2022. In February of that year, the company offered to acquire UniFirst in a transaction valued at $255 per share. That proposal fell through, with the management team at UniFirst deeming the proposed terms as inadequate. In November of 2024, it was proposed once again that Cintas should acquire UniFirst. The offer at that time was higher, coming in at $275 per share. That implied a value of about $5.2 billion. Ultimately, that transaction fell through as well. And in December of last year, Cintas pushed forward with the same offer once again.

It is entirely possible that a transaction between these two companies will never be completed. However, I think that it is certainly possible that a transaction could occur. There is a lot of incentive for Cintas to acquire its smaller competitor, even if it means offering a hefty premium over the current range of offers that we have seen. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for UniFirst for the last three completed fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have largely trended higher. However, things have been a little lumpy. This year, as an example, bottom line performance for UniFirst has become challenged.

As the aforementioned chart shows, net profits and cash flow metrics for the business deteriorated compared to the same time last year. This was in spite of the fact that revenue continued to rise as management succeeds in capturing organic growth through new accounts and improvements when it comes to customer retention. However, costs for the company have been impacted. The weakness seen on the bottom line for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time a year earlier was caused primarily by investments that the business is making in accelerating its growth and continuing forward with its digital transformation activities. This is disappointing to say the least. But we should separate out good costs from bad costs. Any sort of expense item that ultimately creates the prospect of additional growth moving forward should be looked upon in a favorable light even if it means a temporary bit of weakness.

This does not change the fact that 2026 will still be a bit of a difficult year for UniFirst. Management's forecasted revenue for the year of between $2.475 billion and $2.495 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a year over year increase of only 2.2%. However, management believes that earnings per share will come in at only between $6.58 and $6.98. At the midpoint, this would mean net profits of $123.3 million compared to the $148.3 million reported a year earlier. This window of temporary weakness likely helps Cintas from a negotiating standpoint. That is especially true when you consider its own financial track record.

By comparison, Cintas has been much more effective at growing revenue, profits, and cash flows, as time goes on. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the last three completed fiscal years as well as for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. We have seen nothing but consistent upside when it comes to the most important metrics. Clearly, the company is proving successful in expanding its empire. You might think it is curious, then, that Cintas would be interested in acquiring UniFirst. If organic growth is going so well, why even bother with picking up a competitor?

*CTAS Latest Quarters covers 2Qs 2026 vs. 2Qs 2025. UNF Latest Quarters covers Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025.

The most obvious answer is that it would allow immediate scaling. It would reduce competitive pressures as well. But more than that is the prospect of cost savings. Back in December of last year when Cintas announced that it was once again pursuing a purchase of UniFirst, management said that they believed they could capture $375 million of annual run rate synergies within four years of the transaction closing. This might seem improbable. However, as the chart above illustrates, there is a significant disparity when it comes to profit margins when it comes to both businesses.

If we were to assume that UniFirst would be capable of the exact same profit margins that Cintas has captured, this would mean that, instead of the $305.2 million of adjusted operating cash flow the company generated in 2025, it would have generated $566.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA would amount to $639 million compared to the $336.8 million that it ultimately generated. What's interesting is that this is even lower than the $375 million in profit improvement that the company is capable of under the leadership of Cintas. So relying on this kind of assumption is even more conservative than what is likely to be the case if Cintas is accurate.

In the chart above, you can see how UniFirst is being valued at different buyout prices ranging between $275 per share and $300 per share. This is without the prospect of any cost savings. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see what these trading multiples would be if margins for the two companies are equalized. Even without any cost savings, a buyout price of $300 per share is drastically cheaper than what Cintas is going for. And if we do factor in proposed cost savings, the stock looks downright cheap.

This also helps to explain why UniFirst has been so resistant to a transaction occurring. Consider that Cintas is currently trading at a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of 31.2. The EV to EBITDA multiple comes in just a bit lower at 28.4. To put this in perspective, without factoring in potential cost savings, this would translate to a share price for UniFirst of $468 using the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach. And using the EV to EBITDA approach, we are probably looking at somewhere closer to $476 per share. If we factor in opposed cost savings, to match the margin scenario that I outlined already, we are looking at a price of around $869 per share using the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach, and a price of approximately $898 per share using the EV to EBITDA approach.

This represents a massive premium over where UniFirst is trading for at the moment. And in all likelihood, Cintas will have to offer at least $350 per share in order to be taken seriously regarding a buyout. This alone offers massive upside potential for shareholders. But as I detailed already, UniFirst is already attractively priced on an absolute basis without a buyout. Now, if a transaction does occur, and if it is in a more grounded range of between $275 and $300, we can see in the chart below how much upside the company would offer shareholders. This chart shows upside from before the news broke that this transaction was being considered again, and also from a higher price subsequent to the news breaking. Even at $300 per share, which would be exceedingly conservative for a buyout price, we would be looking at upside of 15.4%. Considering that the S&P 500 averages around 10% to 11% per annum, that's a nice bit of upside for such an attractively priced business.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided right now, it is my belief that UniFirst makes for a compelling prospect. If I felt more confidently that a transaction was imminent, I would certainly rate it a Strong Buy right now. But because of how much more of a premium is likely to be required, I think that a deal is not terribly likely to occur right now. That's why I think that settling on a pretty solid Buy rating makes the most sense. And as for Cintas, I remain pessimistic about it from a valuation standpoint, so much so that reaffirming it as a Sell candidate makes the most sense here.

data.title: Granite Ridge Resources: Aiming To Transition To Free Cash Flow Generation In 2027
data.description:
Granite Ridge Resources, Inc. (GRNT) is projected to have a slight cash burn (before dividends) in 2026 at current strip prices. This cash burn may reach around $70 million if Granite Ridge maintains its quarterly dividend at $0.11 per share, though.

While near-term oil prices (CL1:COM, CO1:COM) have soared, Granite Ridge also may have around 75% to 80% of its Q2 2026 oil production hedged, so it needs a more sustained increase in oil prices for its free cash flow to really benefit.

Granite Ridge is continuing to grow its production (with 9% year-over-year total production growth expected) for now but anticipates transitioning to free cash flow generation (along with reduced production growth) in 2027.

I am slightly reducing my estimate of Granite Ridge's value from $7 per share to $6.50 per share due to its increasing debt in 2026 along with lease operating expenses that have been trending higher than expected. Granite Ridge's debt is manageable for now, but its dividend may be at risk if it doesn't start generating substantial free cash flow in 2027.

Q4 2025 Results

Granite Ridge reported 35,120 BOEPD in Q4 2025 production, which was a 10% increase compared to its Q3 2025 production. This slightly exceeded its expectations for high-single-digit quarter-over-quarter growth and allowed it to essentially meet its full-year guidance.

Granite Ridge's oil cut ended up at 50% for 2025, slightly lower than its guidance for 51% to 53% oil. Granite Ridge's oil cut was 49% in Q4 2025. It notes that this was due to some Haynesville wells coming online towards the end of 2025 but believes its oil cut should increase slightly in 2026 based on the mix of wells coming online.

Meanwhile, Granite Ridge's lease operating expenses averaged $7.27 per BOE in 2025, just above its guidance range of $6.25 to $7.25 per BOE. This included $7.72 per BOE in Q4 2025, which was still a reduction compared to the $8.03 per BOE it reported in Q3 2025.

Granite Ridge indicates that its increased focus on the Permian Basin has contributed to the higher lease operating expense.

2026 Outlook

I am modeling Granite Ridge's 2026 production at 35,000 BOEPD (51% oil). This is 9% year-over-year production growth, although 2026 production is averaging around Q4 2025 levels. Granite Ridge's oil production growth may end up around 11%.

At the current strip of $75 WTI oil and $4.05 NYMEX natural gas, I project Granite Ridge to generate $515 million in 2026 revenues after hedges (which have around negative $38 million in value).

A bit over half of Granite Ridge's natural gas production is from the Permian, so that dampens its overall realized prices for natural gas in 2026 with the wide differentials there.

Granite Ridge has approximately 60% of its 2026 oil production and 63% of its 2026 natural gas production hedged. Granite Ridge's oil hedges are primarily collars with a ceiling of around $70.

It may be closer to 75% to 80% hedged on its Q2 2026 oil production, though, so Granite Ridge's benefit from surging near-term oil prices may be relatively modest.

Aiming For Future Free Cash Flow Generation

Granite Ridge anticipates generating free cash flow in 2027 as it slows its production growth and reduces its capex budget.

Granite Ridge's attempts to generate free cash flow in 2027 should benefit from improved Permian natural gas prices as well as its Conduit Power deal. Granite Ridge mentions that it expects its natural gas realizations to improve by $1 to $2 per Mcf for the volumes that are exposed to that contract (starting in 2027).

Notes On Valuation

I previously estimated Granite Ridge's value at $7.00 per share in the long term (after 2026) with $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I am reducing that estimate to $6.50 per share now. On the positive side, Granite Ridge's 2026 cash burn is reduced due to higher oil prices, although this positive impact is reduced by Granite Ridge's hedges (being 60% hedged on oil in 2026 and potentially around 75% to 80% hedged on oil in Q2 2026).

On the negative side, Granite Ridge's lease operating expenses have been trending higher than expected. As well, Granite Ridge's leverage and net debt are expected to increase a bit during 2026.

The lower valuation estimate reflects this being a riskier period for Granite Ridge until it transitions to free cash flow generation that can cover its dividend.

Conclusion

Granite Ridge is continuing to grow production in 2026, which is expected to result in approximately $70 million in cash burn after dividends if it maintains its current dividend. While oil prices have improved, it is also 75% to 80% hedged on projected Q2 2026 oil production, so it will only benefit relatively modestly if the oil price spike doesn't last for longer.

I have reduced my estimate of Granite Ridge's value to $6.50 per share due to its increasing debt as well as higher lease operating expenses. If Granite Ridge can successfully transition to free cash flow generation in 2027 (while covering its dividend), that would improve its value.

data.title: Apartment REITs Part 1: Sector Level Macro
data.description:
This article will discuss the fundamentals of the apartment REIT sector. A follow-up article will look into the valuation and relative opportunity of the individual apartment REITs.

We shall begin with a brief overview of what has transpired in the sector and then follow with an analysis of the fundamental outlook.

The boom and the crash

The pandemic caused various disruptions to the economy, with one of the most notable being the apartment sector.

To keep the economy afloat during the COVID shutdown, two major factors were manipulated:

  • Enormous stimulus
  • Interest rates cut to 0%

The stimulus affects the average American more directly and more immediately, while the impacts of interest rates were substantial but lagged.

The stimulus was so large that people were awash with money. It also came at a time when businesses were closed due to the pandemic, so there was no entertainment on which to spend. The personal savings rate shot up to an unprecedented level in 2020 and early 2021.

Simultaneous with having all this extra money, one’s living space was more important than ever since we were all just stuck at home.

People had the capital and the motivation to rent an apartment. Demand was sudden and enormous, causing a 21% increase in national median rental rates. Median rent shot up from under $1200 to over $1400.

The rental rate peak was in 2022, almost 4 years ago. Apartment List notes that:

“national median rent has now fallen from its 2022 peak by a total of 5.9%.”

Two factors drastically changed the environment:

  1. Consumers ran out of stimulus cash
  2. Unmitigated development

Stimulus checks are not the same as having an income, and employment took a while to fully bounce back after the pandemic. Some people bit off more than they could chew in terms of renting too nice of apartments for their budget.

The historically low vacancy rate in apartments began to surge back up to a more normal level. At first, the higher vacancy was demand driven. The abnormally high demand of 2021 and early 2022 came back down to earth.

In more recent years, the continued increase in vacancy was driven primarily by supply.

That cut to zero interest rates in the pandemic had some serious delayed side effects. When the Fed Funds rate was 0%, developers could borrow money at ridiculously cheap interest rates. Just about any development passes underwriting when the cost of capital is so low.

So developers teed up an enormous amount of multifamily supply, and depending on their individual developer processes as well as the permitting/zoning delays, those developments take 1-3 years to be realized. The development boom started in 2022, with heavy deliveries hitting the market in 2024 and 2025, with the tail end of the development wave hitting in early 2026.

Apartment starts have declined materially, but deliveries remain somewhat elevated.

Demand in 2024 and 2025 was reasonably normal and reasonably healthy, it just wasn’t enough to keep up with the sheer magnitude of new supply.

As a result, vacancy climbed to 7.4% nationally, and rents steadily dropped a total of 5.9% from their 2022 peak.

The apartment REITs have largely suffered during this time period, with the apartment REIT index trading down fully 30% since the start of 2022.

That brings us to today.

Current state of the apartment REITs

The sector is characterized by presently weak fundamentals and somewhat cheap valuation.

  • 7.4% vacancy rate
  • Low rental rates
  • Flattish AFFO growth (2025-2026 guidance)
  • 16.5X mean AFFO multiple
  • 75.4% mean price to NAV

Despite the challenging fundamentals, FFO and AFFO largely held steady for most of the sector. As a result, multiples plummeted. Below is the sector-level trailing FFO multiple.

The sector is clearly cheap by historical standards. Whether that cheapness represents opportunity depends on the forward fundamental outlook.

This is a complex sector with a variety of external factors exerting influence. In the following section I will try my best to provide both the positives and negatives as well as my take on the matter with a caveat that the sheer complexity makes the margin of error larger than a typical forecast.

Apartment fundamental outlook

There are two factors that are significant to apartment REITs:

  1. Rental rate equilibrium
  2. Vacancy rate outlook

I think the first is decidedly bullish, while the other vacancy is a bit more uncertain.

Rental rate equilibrium is significantly higher than current rent

Given the 21% surge in rental rates followed by the 5.9% drop, the chart does not make it inherently obvious whether the 21% was an overshoot to the upside or the 5.9% was an overshoot to the downside.

However, if we combine other data, I think it is clear that rents are abnormally low currently. Given the recent drop, rents are up less than 15% overall since 2020. As this was a high inflation period, real rents are actually down.

Rental rates look even lower when we compare them to the alternative: homeownership.

Homeownership costs have surged. I think most people are aware of the roughly 30% increase in home prices.

However, there are so many more factors that have made homeownership incredibly difficult to afford.

The higher price means a larger mortgage, and people are now paying a higher rate on that mortgage.

Mortgage rates have come down a bit in recent months, but are still very high compared to 2021.

Higher home prices also mean larger property tax bills. HOA fees have increased. Repair bills have gone up.

Perhaps the most painful change of all is the surge in insurance costs.

In combination, these factors have increased the average cost of home ownership to $2,035 in 2024. This is using Census data, so it is a bit delayed.

“Median monthly owner costs increased 3.8% from 2023 to 2024, more than the increase of 3.0% from 2022 ($1,902) to 2023. This increase was primarily driven by higher mortgage costs and insurance fees.”

It is even higher now, as insurance costs have continued to increase since 2024.

In comparison, national median asking rent is $1,357. Renting is currently much cheaper than owning. Even if one has the money for a down payment, renting is the more economical option on a run-rate basis.

Historically this has not been the case. In fact, homeownership has often been the more affordable route.

Given the extreme gap of affordability between owning and renting, we see one of the following as necessary to get to equilibrium:

  1. Homeownership costs come down
  2. Rental rates go up

While low demand for homes could in theory take prices down, there is a limit to how low they can go, and that limit is based on the cost to build, repair, and insure homes.

Homebuilders are already stretching to get their costs as low as possible to try to stimulate demand. Lennar’s (LEN) margins have contracted substantially, and the same is true of most of the builders.

Note that the above is gross margin, with net margin in the single digits in many cases.

It is approaching a point where homes simply cannot be delivered at a significantly lower price point.

Thus, I think it is far more likely that apartment rents will come up.

Rental rates have been held down artificially by the intense lease-up period of the development wave. Apartments are built at 0% occupancy, and to get rents flowing, they want to lease up as fast as possible. This often means rental rates that are lower than true market rates as well as concessions like free month(s) of rent.

With the development wave subsiding, there are soon to be fewer new apartments in their initial lease-up phase. This will allow rental rates to move up toward equilibrium, which we believe is significantly higher, to bring the cost of renting more in line with the cost of homeownership.

High vacancy could be sustaining

The current 7.4% vacancy rate is above recent norms and may remain for a while. Even though new supply is tapering back down, demand growth is also likely to be a bit subdued due to the lack of population growth.

Population growth has slowed to 0.5% as of the January Census data (which covers through July 2025).

This is largely due to much tighter immigration policy, leaving domestic birth rates as just about the sole driver of population growth.

Anemic population growth suggests incremental apartment demand would largely have to come from a tick up in household formation rates.

Household formation – offsetting forces

Given the widespread complaints about the affordability of housing, one would presume that there is pent-up household formation. In other words, there should be a significant number of people who want to form households but haven’t yet for cost reasons.

While apartments are clearly cheaper than homeownership,it turns out the most affordable option is to do neither.

  • Children might choose to live with parents longer.
  • Couch surfing
  • High numbers of roommates

Intuitively this idea of pent-up demand makes a lot of sense, yet it is somehow not supported by the data. The number of households in the U.S. has continually risen and even increased as a percentage of the population.

This would imply that there is NOT an abnormally large contingent of pent-up household formation.

Perhaps there are other factors offsetting this:

  • People are remaining single longer, which means two households instead of 1
  • People are having fewer kids, which means average household size should be smaller

I suspect there is some degree of pent-up demand for household formation. It is just difficult to measure how big that might be.

As such, it is somewhat unclear how much incremental demand there will be. There is some demand from household formation and reasonably limited demand from population growth. In my opinion these will roughly offset the remaining supply pipeline, so I think vacancy rates will remain elevated in the near to medium term and then slowly drop to maybe 6%.

Overall take on apartment sector

Fundamentals are a bit weak currently but look to improve going forward, driven by equilibrant rental rates being significantly higher than current rents.

Occupancy is a bit tougher, with vacancy likely to remain elevated for a bit. However, apartment REITs tend to be better managed than the national average, and REIT occupancy rates are substantially higher than the 92.6% national average.

Given the sharp discount to NAV at which apartment REITs trade and improving fundamentals, I believe it's worth investing in the apartment REIT sector. It may require some patience, but the moves could be swift once the inflection point becomes more visible to the market. As such, we are limping in now and plan to build our allocation to the sector over time.

The follow-up, part 2, will discuss which apartment REITs are best positioned within the sector.

data.title: The February Jobs Drop: Outlier Or New Trend
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this morning, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the February employment report. The report surprised markets, showing a job loss of 92,000 for the month of February, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.4%. This report, combined with previous reports being revised downward, may be hinting at renewed weakness in the labor market.

Jobless Claims Hinted At Some Trouble

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover (JOLTS) report has been delayed due to the government shutdown, so investors had limited data leading up to the report. Fortunately, the weekly jobless claims report was still released on schedule. On an initial claims basis, there were no signs in February that the labor market was weakening. Initial weekly claims remained under 215,000 for three weeks during the month, which is below the majority of the last 52 weeks.

The continued claims told a slightly different story. While continuing claims are lower than where they were in most of the past year, they did spike towards the end of February to the highest level of the month. At 1.87 million, continuing claims were 30,000 higher over the four-week period and 50,000 higher than the prior week. While we do not get the 2/28 data until next week, I am expecting to see this jump to over 1.9 million. With jobless claims showing modest gains, it’s safe to assume that the next two JOLTS reports, which will be released over a three-week period, will show a notable slowing of hiring in the economy.

The Signs Of Weakness

There are a few indicators that show the labor market may be experiencing ongoing losses. First, the overall job creation numbers since the beginning of 2025 have been disappointing. Over the last twelve months, the economy has only created 156,000 jobs and has experienced five months of net job losses. Looking at the trend, it appears as if the labor market is taking a “three steps forward, two steps back” approach, with the possibility of it worsening in the coming months.

In addition to weak job creation, the average duration of unemployment exceeded 25 weeks in February for the first time in the post-pandemic business cycle. By comparison, the average duration of unemployment before the pandemic was around 22 weeks. The rise in average duration shows that newly unemployed workers are having a tougher time finding new work.

Another indicator showing weakness is average hours worked. Following the pandemic, average hours worked rose to levels not seen in recent memory as current workers were strained by labor supply shortages. Today, not only has that trend reversed, but average hours worked have remained at levels not consistently seen since 2010.

Conflicting Indicators Of Strength

Despite the loss of jobs and some of the underlying trends, there is some data pointing to stability in the labor market. First is the overall unemployment rate. While the unemployment rate rose to 4.4% in February, it is currently sitting at the level projected by the Federal Reserve for the end of this year. The Fed will update its economic projections later this month, but I am expecting to see unemployment expectations hold at this level, which is considered slightly below full employment.

Another indicator that showed surprising strength in February was the U6 rate, which dropped. The U6 rate is considered the broadest measure of unemployment, with part-time workers seeking full-time employment included in its measure. In February, the U6 rate dropped 0.2% to 7.9%, the lowest level since July. The U6U3 spread, which measures slack amongst the marginally employed, dropped to 3.5%. Whether this indicator is a leading or trailing indicator is hard to tell, but I don’t believe this spread will remain intact; therefore, between U6 and the unemployment rate, one will likely have a big move in March.

Another interesting surprise was average hourly earnings. Despite the softness in the labor market, employees are still seeing healthy wage growth, with average hourly earnings rising by 3.8% on a year-over-year basis. The increase in wages demonstrates that employers are (at the very least) valuing existing labor enough to provide pay increases that avoid turnover.

Conclusion

While the surprise weakness in the February jobs report may have caught investors off guard, I’m not expecting much from the Fed in the immediate term. With the unemployment rate at their target, I don’t see the central bank acting on its forecasted cut in March. This may change if more losses and higher unemployment ensue in future months, but for now, we are in a wait-and-see mode.

data.title: A Jobs Bloodbath Affirms Our Down View Of The Economy
data.description:
NEW YORK (March 6) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) today reported 92,000 jobs lost, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey.

The number was a catastrophic miss from the 65,000 jobs that markets had anticipated. The private payroll number showed 86,000 jobs lost after taking account of 6,000 government jobs lost. December and January revisions resulted in an additional 69,000 jobs lost.

For the three months from December, 2025, average monthly jobs creation has been less than 6,000 jobs per month, affirming our view in prior jobs and GDP reports that the economy is slowing.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed even worse than the Establishment Survey, showing 185,000 jobs lost.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate were 4.4% and 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Just 18,000 people joined the civilian labor force, the Labor Force Participation Rate fell nonetheless, to just 62%.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of November and December Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

As you can see, jobs data for February was a veritable blood bath, with virtually no bright spots in the current months' (green) bars. So let’s move on to the status of the…

Economy Generally

The onset of the Iran War has significantly altered the outlook for the economy. Gasoline and diesel fuel prices have increased significantly, according to this chart adopted from AAA:
