Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) have also spiked to their highest level in a decade, as seen in the SA Chart linked here.

Those higher fuel prices will be felt throughout the economy.

Though there is some debate, it is generally observed that spikes in fuel prices tend to be disinflationary, and even deflationary, at least in the government statistics roundup because the Fed’s reported preferred measure of inflation, Core PCE inflation, excludes food and fuel. Since consumers’ disposable income will likely be eaten up by higher fuel prices, less is left for them to spend on consumer staples and discretionaries. (We already see some of this in the disposable personal income figures where spending has exceeded disposable income, in the "Other Data Points" section of this report.)

The market has largely gone “risk:off” because of the war. The global economy has been negatively affected as well, as Iran continues to threaten transshipments of oil from the Persian Gulf by bottling up the Strait of Hormuz. It’s being reported today that Kuwait has ceased production because its oil storage facilities are full. Iraq has cut production as well, and Qatar is threatening the same.

One-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supply has been cut off and the cash flow to the aforementioned countries has been cut off. (See the important "GEOPOLITICAL NOTE" below, as it could substantially affect the economy.)

The economic consequences of the war highlights that the Fed has botched rates for several meetings and that private, business-oriented hiring - outside the healthcare and education sector - has slowed considerably.

We note, too, that the job opening rate, per the Beveridge Curve, is 3.9%, the lowest it has been since May, 2017.

The “green shoots” we had hoped to see coming in the latter quarters of this year from capex from re-shoring may have dissipated because of the Supreme Court’s tariff decision. We may still see significant gains in the construction sector coming from AI data center construction, but local opposition to such builds will delay job growth from them into later quarters or years.. That said, we note that the ISM Manufacturing Index, discussed and linked below, is in its second month of “growth” after a near year of "contraction.

Average jobs creation over the last three months has been a paltry 6,000 jobs per month.

Core CPE inflation has stepped up somewhat in December, to 3.0%. Part of that has been rough winter weather in population centers in the Northeast and northern Central States; some has occurred because of major healthcare worker strikes. The Labor Force Participation Rate was just 62%.

It should not be lost on the Fed that the labor force participation rate is likely to increase in 2026 due to work requirements for SNAP recipients. (Work requirements for Medicaid start in 2027.)

While the additional workers might be absorbed from the generous fiscal policy of the “Big Beautiful Bill,” high interest rates will inhibit the ability of small and medium sized businesses - the traditional engines of job growth - to access the capital that will allow them to make use of first year depreciation, lower hiring costs (from tax free tips), etc.

The Fed's discount rate should probably be about 2.25 to 2.75%, about 100 bps lower than present. Even if it was that low, the discount rate has little effect on longer-term rates, which are the problem for jobs creation.

We reiterate our desire to see the incoming Fed chair, Kevin Warsh, engage in yield curve control so that longer-term rates can be reduced. Alternatively (or, perhaps, in conjunction with that monetary policy), we would like to see Congress enact fiscal policy that would exempt Treasurys entirely or partially from Federal Income Tax. Yield curve control sets longer term rates within thresholds set by the Fed, with the Fed buying and selling to keep such rates within the targeted range. Exempting Treasury interest from Federal Income Tax would increase demand, raise bond prices, and thereby lower rates. In addition, since rates would decrease, it would help ameliorate some of the deficit since the interest on the federal debt could be reduced. (We did this during World War I to control rates and inflation, so it’s not unprecedented. It would, however, obviously “horn in” on the municipal bond market, so that rates there would increase and cause higher yields since they would not be as attractive as the federal bonds.)

PROGNOSTICATIONS

We reiterate our view of the last several months that the data point to a slowing economy. PCE spending is slowing; however, we think there may be substantially increased GDI owing to the Big Beautiful Bill, but that won’t be reflected, likely, until at least 2026Q2 or Q3. That would make the mid-term elections a jump ball.

The Trump Administration needs to better manage expectations if it expects to maintain Congress in November. Otherwise, given the mood of the Democrat caucus, it’s almost certain the president would, again, be impeached and his agenda scuttled.

The president also needs to message his war aims and casus belli far better than he has so far. This morning, we learned that the war aim is “unconditional surrender”.

But that objective should have come in a national address, not a “Truth” post. Such an address would have had the president explain how unconditional surrender will be achieved without U.S. ground troops or an air campaign that does not include nuclear weapons.

I cannot think of a historical instance where “unconditional surrender” - or even regime change - has been effected solely in an air campaign. (Kosovo, possibly? In Japan, we used two nuclear weapons and we still left Hirohito in place, despite his war crimes.)

In view of today’s release, we are downgrading our estimate of 2026 Q1 to be 1.25%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the same for 2026Q2 for now, but it will depend on the length of the war. We expect the Fed will stand pat with interest rates at its March meeting.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 1.4% in 2025Q4, according to the Advance Estimate released February 20th. Core GDP, or real final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 2.4%.

The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index increased 2.9 percent. Excluding food and energy prices, the PCE price index increased 3.0 percent. Core PCE inflation is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index (PMI), for February, released Monday, fell slightly to 52.4 from 52.6. This is the second month of a growth trend from an prior 11 month contraction. The February Services Index, released Wednesday, printed at 56.1, up 2.3 points from January.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, JOLTS, for December, was released February 5th with 386,000 fewer job openings than in November.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in December were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,448,000. This is 4.3 percent above the revised November rate of 1,388,000, but is 2.2 percent below the December 2024 rate of 1,480,000. Privately-owned housing starts in December were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,404,000. This is 6.2 percent (±10.7 percent)* above the revised November estimate of 1,322,000, but is 7.3 percent (±14.0 percent)* below the December 2024 rate of 1,514,000.

Personal income increased $86.2 billion (0.3 percent at a monthly rate) in December, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $75.7 billion (0.3 percent), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $91.0 billion (0.4 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for March printed at 47.5, continuing a down trend..

GEOPOLITICAL NOTE: The reduction in cash flow and employment in the oil producing countries affected by the war could have adverse geopolitical consequences in some of those countries that could have a catastrophic affect on the world economy. We all remember the populist uprisings of the Arab Spring. Oil money has largely pacified the lower-income, less-educated, populations of the oil producing countries, who are the overwhelming majority of their populations. There is at least some risk of popular uprisings in those countries should the oil shut-down last terribly long. Such uprisings might also be triggered more imminently over water if Iran hits the many desalination plants that serve these desert areas. We should not underestimate the strategic value to China of inciting such anti-government movements among the oil-producing countries, several of which are US allies and hosts to forward US military bases.

______________________________________________

AUTHOR'S NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: Carrefour: Like Walmart, But With A 7% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Yesterday, I was looking at some Walmart (WMT) charts and saw this bizarre thing:

Today, Walmart's forward P/E ratio is 43x, which is already high considering its average of 27x. But there's a huge gap when we consider the Nasdaq (QQQ), and this shows how the market is (heavily) rewarding some stocks.

But then I had the idea of checking out Walmart's global peers. I know that domestic peers, such as Costco (COST), are just as expensive or even more expensive, and basically that's the only player as big as Walmart. But it turns out that some names like Carrefour SA (CRRFY) (CRERF) aren't just a little cheaper; they're much cheaper than Walmart, which shows how the market is also neglecting some regions.

We aren't looking at a fragile player in the supermarket industry or, for instance, a big player in a volatile region such as Latin America. Carrefour is actually a European behemoth, but with a very low valuation (and with a dividend yield of 7.4%). I'm starting my coverage of Carrefour with a buy.

Carrefour Isn't A Small Company

I think that before delving into Carrefour's risks, it's worth looking at its big numbers, precisely because the single-digit P/E makes it look like it's a dying company or one with very high risks, which definitely isn’t the case.

Carrefour has about 15,000 stores, and check the image below; they're a leader or second in core regions. They're a leader only in Brazil, and the market is still very fragmented, so it has a lot of capacity for expansion. Last year, Carrefour had €91B+ in revenue. Of course, it's still far from Walmart's size (around 87% lower), but it's still a big figure, especially in the European context.

Still comparing it with Walmart, Carrefour doesn’t have a giant ecosystem yet, but we can't say it's just a simple supermarket. Private labels already have very high penetration, which helps with margins, and e-commerce is in a good momentum, not to mention portfolio optimization moves such as the delisting of Brazilian Carrefour.

This wouldn't be that appealing if Carrefour's bottom line (mainly cash flow) weren't already quite solid. In 2025, EBITDA was €4.5B, with very low growth of around just a little higher than 3% (ignoring the FX impact). The top line was kind of similar, growing a little lower than that on a like-for-like basis (they call it LFL, but it's similar to comparable sales or same-store sales).

It may not seem like much growth, and it really isn't, but in terms of cash flow, it becomes more interesting. Net free cash flow, after expansion capex and everything else, Carrefour left €1.3B in free cash flow, which for a company worth around €10 billion is already an absurd FCF yield. What impresses me most is that Carrefour's management wants to achieve around €5B accumulated net free cash flow (I will call it NFCF or FCF from now on) in the next three years, basically three years of FCF, which would already pay half of the entire Carrefour's market cap.

Carrefour also isn't a high-leverage company; the net debt/adjusted EBITDA is lower than 1x.

Carrefour Stock Is Dirt Cheap

Taking the figures I just mentioned, it's quite clear that Carrefour's case is very appealing in terms of valuation. It's a cash cow, has huge free cash flow linked to real assets, with some inflation protection because it focuses on low prices and has scale (kind of like Walmart), and still manages to offer a free cash flow yield of 12%. If we look at Walmart's operating cash flow yield (yes, I'm not even taking free cash flow), it's already close to 4%.

If you take other multiples, it's also a very large distortion. The forward P/E is 8.5x, while WMT's is 38x and Costco's is 45x. The EV-to-EBITDA is less than 6x, and for the two American behemoths, it's more than 20x.

Besides, we're also talking about a deep value case: Carrefour has 14 billion in real estate, with ~9 billion in France and 3 billion in Brazil. For this reason, Carrefour's P/B ratio is less than 1x. And this real estate has been a very interesting asset in recent years, as management highlighted in the recent call:

“It comes out that the value of our real estate portfolio for France, Spain and Brazil amounts to EUR 14.2 billion at the end of 2025. Over the past four years, the value of this portfolio has grown by EUR 2.8 billion or plus 25%.”

I'm not saying that Carrefour doesn't deserve a discount or that Walmart doesn't deserve a premium, but this gap is too big. If Carrefour traded at a P/E of 12x and Walmart at a P/E of 25x, it would seem quite reasonable to me. But Carrefour certainly doesn't seem so bad to me that it should be offering a dividend yield of 7%, a double-digit free cash flow yield, and everything else.

Risks And Bottom Line

I have to mention that I really don't expect a re-rating on Carrefour stock; it won't trade for the same multiples as WMT stock. There are some points that you will notice about Carrefour's case that make it less comfortable. As it operates in several geographies at the same time, the tax rate is much higher, with the normative rate in 2025 being 27.9% and the effective rate being 33%.

Then we have the Brazil "problem." It's not a real problem, but about 20% of sales are in the Brazilian operations, and we're talking about a country with a volatile environment, inflation, high-interest rates, and other matters. That's why debt is not a concern, but Carrefour's interest rates are quite high. Net financial expenses in 2025 were €578 million.

And in this geographical mix alone, which is closely related to inflation, interest rates, and regulation, Walmart already has a significant advantage. Mainly because we must remember that in France, there's a strong issue related to unions, regulation, and even certain political pressure regarding price increases. Also, the demographic dynamics in Europe compared to the US are not as favorable.

So it really is a more complex and riskier case, not risky to the point of being disrupted, but to the point where the scenario is more nebulous - perhaps more difficult to grow; perhaps the portfolio optimizations will take a little longer to have a bottom-line benefit, and this uncertainty needs to be considered when buying the stock.

But still, as it's a very resilient company with a strong brand, a large geographical presence, real assets (and real estate), and a very undervalued valuation, my rating is a buy for Carrefour stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy Oracle's 'Bubble Fears' Before Next Week's Q3 Earnings Report
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Oracle (ORCL) stock may have finally struck a bottom.

The stock has been struggling since September 2025, and its miseries did not end in 2026 either, as the stock fell another ~20% on a YTD basis.

This was driven by investor sentiment souring on AI capex as the four other hyperscalers that include Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) significantly raised their capex projections for FY26 in their latest earnings reports. The only thing that was different this time was that these companies would also take a severe hit to their free cash flow and increasingly tap into the debt markets, similar to Oracle to fund their capex goals, raising AI bubble fears.

As a result, we have seen investors abandon the hyperscaler trade in 2026.

Up until now.

Over the last week or so, these companies are once again outperforming the S&P 500, even as geopolitical uncertainty worsens.

The way I see it, this could be tied to OpenAI's $110B funding announcement from two weeks ago, which gives investors more confidence that it can fulfill its $250B compute contract with Microsoft and $300B compute contract with Oracle.

Not only that, but even Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) Jensen Huang also firmly pointed out that $3-$5T of capex buildout over the next several years is necessary for every data center to transition to accelerated computing that would require them to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

So, while the revenue materialization risks still remain, along with the power constraint introducing a physical ceiling on how fast the revenue gap can close, investors have been rotating back into hyperscalers once again, including Oracle, which is up 9.5% over the last five days, outperforming the other four.

The company is slated to report its Q3 FY26 earnings on March 10, when revenue is expected to accelerate to 19.17%, with no signs of a slowdown after that until FY29. With the company taking certain measures to stabilize its CDS (Credit Default Swap) spreads along with its layoff announcement yesterday to boost profitability, I will reiterate my “buy” rating at current levels.

A Quick Recap Of What I Said Earlier

In my previous post on Oracle, I pointed out three things from Oracle’s Q2 FY26 earnings that fed the AI bears exactly what they wanted to hear.

First, its Cloud Revenue (Infrastructure + Applications) revenue missed analyst estimates for the second consecutive quarter.

Second, the earnings per share beat was largely attributed to a one-time pre-tax gain of $2.7B from the sale of its interest in the Ampere chip company.

Third, Oracle’s capex came in $3.7B higher than what analysts were expecting at $12B. On top of that, Oracle management further raised their full-year capex to $50B, up from their previous estimate of $35B, which would now amount to 75% of total revenue, which is significantly higher than the other four hyperscalers that include Microsoft, Meta, Google, and Amazon, even after these companies have substantially raised their full-year FY26 capex projections as well as of the latest quarter.

Therefore, despite Oracle demonstrating 15% QoQ growth in backlog, investors chose to focus on their free cash flow, which had gone from a positive $11.2B to a negative -$13.2B in just six quarters, suggesting a lot of debt-fueled capex ahead, along with customer concentration risk from OpenAI, accounting for 60% of Oracle’s backlog.

During the previous quarter, Oracle increased their long-term debt from $82B to over $99B. Given their significantly higher debt-to-EBITDA ratio compared to that of other hyperscalers, their CDS spread is also much wider, reflecting investor concerns on ROI on AI capex.

The AI Capex Narrative Came Under A Significant Pressure Test In 2026.

Since Oracle’s earnings at the end of 2025, we have heard from Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, which have all significantly increased their FY26 capex to grow roughly 70%, 97%, 52%, and 73% YoY, respectively.

In other words, we are now looking at a combined $680B in AI capex for 2026 for the four companies above (excluding Oracle), which is expected to be approximately double that of the FY25 figure.

Yet, despite these companies beating their revenue and earnings estimates in the latest quarter, all four are down for the year, underperforming the S&P 500.

As this X thread points out, this is all about how AI capex is altering the free cash flow mechanics at these companies, and investors are not too happy about it. Here’s how the author framed the math below.

“The math: last year the four hyperscalers generated $200 billion in combined free cash flow. This year they’re guiding to nearly $700 billion in capex. Amazon alone expects $200 billion in spending, and Morgan Stanley projects its free cash flow goes negative by $17 billion. Alphabet’s FCF is forecast to drop ~90%, from $73.3 billion to $8.2 billion. Alphabet did a $25 billion bond sale in November, then another $20 billion sale in February. Their long-term debt quadrupled in 2025 to $46.5 billion.”

In other words, these companies that once used to fund their AI capex using their own free cash flow are now expected to tap into the debt market as revenue fails to realize fast enough in the short term.

Meanwhile, Michael Burry has also been mass publishing on the depreciation cliff that these companies face and that the markets haven’t priced in yet. According to him, reported operating income may be inflated by as much as 20% at companies like Oracle and Meta given the accounting gap between Nvidia’s GPU architecture cycles and their depreciation timelines.

Not only that, assuming a 20% annual depreciation on $2T in estimated capex by 2030 across these five hyperscalers, it amounts to $400B per year, which exceeds the combined profits these companies generated in 2025.

Aside from the revenue materialization risks that these companies face today, a power constraint also introduces a physical ceiling on how fast the revenue gap can close. According to this post written by Robert P. Ballan, power generation buildout takes 3-7 years, which is much longer than chip manufacturing cycles. In other words, there is a probability that we run into a scenario whereby the temporal mismatch could translate into "billions of dollars of the most advanced AI hardware ever built may sit dark — depreciating on balance sheets, servicing debt, generating zero revenue — while waiting for power infrastructure that is years behind.”

Given the overarching risks facing the future of free cash flow generation at these companies, investors abandoned the hyperscaler trade altogether in 2026, as you can see in their YTD performance below.

A Sigh Of Relief After OpenAI’s $110B Funding Round

However, we may be finally at an inflection point in the AI capex narrative that turned south at the start of the year.

It started with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang defending the $3-4T of AI capex buildout over the next several years, as he believes it to be necessary for every data center to transition to accelerated computing to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

“In this new world of AI, compute equals revenues. Without compute, there’s no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there’s no way to grow revenues. [...] I am certain at this point that we’ve reached the inflection point.”

As this X thread points out, this isn’t discretionary capex that the hyperscalers are investing in. This is survival capex as we enter a regime where the companies that deliver the lowest cost per token per watt win.

Furthermore, what may have also helped improve investor sentiment on the AI capex narrative could be OpenAI’s announcement of an $110B funding round on February 27, which is more than double the size of its last raise a year ago.

A week prior to that, OpenAI bumped its 5-year revenue forecast up by 27%, targeting $280B in revenue by 2030 as they hit a new peak of 910M Weekly Active Users, with GPT 5.1 and 5.2 updates re-sparking growth. This would mean that OpenAI would now need to grow its revenues by 70% CAGR.

To this, BNP Paribas analyst Stefan Slowinski pointed out in a note to clients that both Microsoft and Oracle should benefit from OpenAI’s latest funding round as the company is now well positioned to fund a $250B compute contract with Microsoft and a $300B compute contract with Oracle, which I agree with.

Is The Downside Risk Fully Priced In?

Oracle is scheduled to report its Q3 FY26 earnings next week on March 10, where revenue is expected to grow at an accelerated rate of 19.67%, followed by no signs of slowdown at least until FY29.

To that, the good news is that despite the tremendous downward volatility in the stock price, there have been no downward revisions to the company’s forward revenue growth estimates.

In all of this, what has also helped the stock find some footing is its CDS spread, which has declined by several basis points after it announced a plan to use a $20B equity program to supplement $22.5B in bond issuance for its capital raise in 2026, subsiding fears of a credit downgrade.

Just to reiterate, when it comes to Oracle’s long-term projections, management expects their OCI to accelerate by 77% in FY26 to $18B and scale to $144B by FY30, allowing it to capture roughly 16% of the cloud market, thus putting it at a similar scale to the next closest hyperscaler by FY29.

However, what is important from here onwards is management actually delivering at or above their projections. Any further misses on their top-line growth estimates would lead to serious trouble ahead.

When it comes to profitability, the picture is mixed, as the company has been receiving several downgrades over the past 1-3M period.

Plus, yesterday, the company announced its plans to reduce its workforce by thousands of employees across multiple divisions that could be implemented as soon as this month, which could help stabilize the overall profitability to a certain extent.

The stock is currently trading at just 21x its projected FY26 earnings per share, which is expected to grow 21.77% YoY. The multiple compresses down to just 14.7x when taking the company’s projected FY28 earnings per share into account, which is substantially lower than the 10-year average S&P 500 multiple of 18.8, as well as the price multiples of the four other hyperscalers. This is also unlike a “bubble” environment where you would typically see valuations of companies spike.

So, what is the “right” multiple at which Oracle stock should be trading?

To be honest, it is hard to determine one at the moment given the elevated investor skepticism towards the predictability of its cash flows and ROI on AI capex. I believe the first solid sign of a bottom will be determined when the stock closes above its 200DMA at $220 per share, which represents an upside of 40% from current levels.

Meanwhile, the average price target on Wall Street currently sits at $257 per share, which represents an upside potential of 63% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

So, are we finally out of the woods yet?

It's too early to say that.

While investor sentiment on AI capex has been improving over the past week or so after OpenAI’s funding announcement and Nvidia’s Huang defending the current scale of the infrastructure buildout, it is still important to note that the mismatch between investor expectation of AI revenue realization and when it actually materializes may induce several waves of AI bubble fears that can create downward volatility for Oracle’s stock.

In the medium term, however, I believe that if Oracle demonstrates a stronger than expected beat and raise in its Q3 FY26 earnings, it could set the stage for a significant re-rating higher in the stock. As a result, I will maintain my “buy” rating on the stock at current levels.

data.title: Markets Are Underpricing Inflation Risk - The February CPI Preview
data.description:
Consensus Expectations - Near The 2% Target

The U.S. BLS is set to release the February CPI inflation report on March 11th. The consensus expectations are:

  • The core CPI to increase by 0.2% MoM, which would be less than 0.3% MoM in January, and keep the annual core CPI unchanged at 2.5%, and
  • The headline CPI inflation is to increase by 0.2% MoM, unchanged from January; however, due to base effects, this would push the annual CPI down from 2.5% to 2.4%.

Thus, practically, based on these expectations, CPI inflation is very near the Fed's 2% target. Note, CPI inflation is generally around 50 bpt higher than the Fed's preferred PCE inflation measure.

Other Inflation Measures Disagree

However, other official inflation measures show a different picture. The PPI inflation has been actually heating up. Below is the chart of the annual Producer Prices Final Demand Less Foods, Energy, and Trade Services. This measure of PPI is at 3.4%, and it has been rising since June 2025, with the spike in October 2025, which is still in progress. This is likely due to tariffs.

Below is the chart of the Core PCE Price Index Annual Change, and this is the Fed's preferred inflation measure. The core PCE is at 3%, with the spike in December 2025. Note, the core PCE is supposed to be at 2%.

Thus, a cooling CPI inflation is not consistent with other official measures of inflation. Note, CPI inflation is heavily influenced by shelter inflation (rent), which has been falling, and that could be the difference.

Based on the chart below, the national average rent was $1,626 per month, which is an increase of 0.4% over the last year - the lowest increase since 2016. Rents are falling as the vacancy rate is rising, currently at 8.6%, the highest since 2016. This is likely to continue due to slower population growth given the immigration policy, in addition to an increase in multifamily housing construction.

Soft-Data Inflation Indicators

Soft-data inflation indicators are currently mixed.

The ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid Index shows a major spike in inflation for February, and this is consistent with PPI inflation, possibly reflecting the tariffs.

However, the ISM Services Prices Paid Index shows a dip in inflation for February, which actually started in November. Note, the Service Price Index is still well above 50 at 63, which points to high inflation; however, the trend is disinflationary.

The Logistic Managers Index LMI Transportation Prices also shows a spike in transportation inflation for February, and the spike started in October 2025. This is also consistent with a hot PPI inflation.

At the same time, consumer inflation expectations have been falling consistently since May 2025, now at 3.2%, based on Michigan 1-Year Inflation expectations; and this is consistent with a cooling CPI inflation.

Thus, the soft data points to 1) rising inflation in manufacturing, with higher transportation costs, which is consistent with rising PPI inflation, and 2) disinflation in consumer prices due to falling rents, but also possibly due to a weaker labor market, which is consistent with a cooling CPI inflation. In a sense, this is a stagflationary picture.

Market Measures Of Inflation

Investors are generally pricing in well-anchored long-term inflation.

The 5-year/5-year forward inflation expectations are very low at 2.1%. This is a measure of expected inflation over the five-year period that begins five years from today.

The 10Y Breakeven Inflation is at 2.31%, which also reflects a well-anchored long-term inflation expectation. This measure is derived as the difference between the 10Y Treasury nominal yield and the 10Y Treasury TIPS yield (real rate). The measure is well below the critical 2.5% level.

The 5-year Breakeven Inflation is more elevated at 2.5%, but still within the normal range. Note, this market measure of inflation will generally spike first if there is an inflationary shock.

Thus, investors give the Fed vote of confidence, expecting that long-term inflation will remain well-anchored.

Note, the Fed "panics" only when these market measures of inflation start to rise. In fact, the Fed started to hike in 2022 only after the Breakeven Inflation spiked. Thus, it is important to monitor the bond market reaction to the CPI inflation.

The Unfolding Energy Price Shock

The release of CPI inflation will likely be overlooked by investors because there is an unfolding energy price shock with the U.S.-Iran war, and the outcome of this war changes everything.

Currently, WTI Crude Oil (CL1:COM) is trading near $90/barrel. We are in a nightmare scenario where Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz and is bombing the regional energy infrastructure. If this situation persists for a few more weeks, the price of oil could spike well above $100, possibly even above $200 - and currently there is no end in sight.

The spike in oil prices could cause massive inflation over the near term, but also massive deflation if a global recession follows.

The oil futures market expects the war to last for about 4–5 weeks, with prices returning to the mid-70s by June/July.

The Macro Picture

The current spike in oil prices is a part of an unfolding deglobalization trend, which includes global fragmentation into blocks, trade protectionism, imperialism with a hunt for critical commodities, and physical wars. This macro environment will likely create more frequent spikes in various commodity prices, food prices, and supply chain bottlenecks - all pointing to higher long-term inflation.

Thus, investors are underpricing long-term inflation by assuming a well-anchored inflation consistent with the Fed's 2% target.

Other issues to consider include the sustainability of the U.S. Dollar (DXY) as the global reserve currency in this environment and the sustainability of the U.S. fiscal deficit in a situation where global demand for Treasuries could decrease. Both issues point to higher inflation.

Implications

Financial markets have been remarkably resilient and stable despite the turbulent macro environment, and the main reason is stability in the bond market, or specifically due to expectations of well-anchored long-term inflation consistent with the Fed's 2% target.

Thus, the release of the February CPI report will matter only if it moves the bond market.

The Federal Funds futures are currently pricing a policy normalization in 2026, with the Fed cutting one or two times. The 10Y Treasuries are pricing solid growth and anchored inflation. A "hot" CPI could tilt the Federal Funds futures to price one cut, and this could cause some volatility in the stock market.

However, in my opinion, the February CPI release will be overshadowed by the unfolding current energy price spike.

In my opinion, investors are ignoring 1) the inflationary pressures in the PPI and PCE inflation measures, 2) the current energy price shock, and 3) the longer-term inflationary macro picture - thus underpricing inflation risk.

In this situation, 10Y Yields (US10Y) should be higher, closer to 5%, and the Fed should not be thinking about cutting rates.
